title: Intro Call — Justin Massey
meeting_title: Intro Call — Marcel Santilli & Justin Massey

transcript.text: What's going on, sir?
This meeting is being recorded.
You're on mute.
How are you?
I guess your stat, your thing makes me come in on mute.
How are you doing?
Pretty good, man.
Not bad.
been crazy, but in a good way, so I can't complain, yeah.
Nice.
Testing out some note-takers there.
got Fathom, and GrowthX, and Adio.
Testing them all out, man.
Do you like Adio?
My subscription for HubSpot expires in one week, and I haven't, I'm not renewing my HubSpot subscription.
What do think of Adio?
The parts I love, I love, and the parts that are missing things are deal-breakers.
That's the way to think about it.
Oh, no.
Everything else, they've
It's imperfect.
It's just that the things that they just haven't done at all are just deal breakers.
Give me a high level on like one of the things that you're like a deal breaker on.
Like you can't, like no timestamping on anything.
And so you can't really report to the board on like things that you normally would need to report to the board on, like your pipeline or like your funnel stuff, you know?
That sounds, they've got to have timestamps.
Okay.
All right.
Cool.
Thanks.
I, yeah, I, I was so annoyed when my VP of ops was like, we're going to search up.
So I was like, what, why?
It's like, and then that wasn't the only thing, but it was just mostly like some, some basic things that were, were very like, okay.

Like if you're using this as a, as a notion with more CRM stuff within, but then like, I think I'm just going to keep audio and use it as my personal CEO CRM because like my chief of staff and my EA need access to see like the interactions and have like places where we put notes on, on, on people on the network.
And, you know, I'm constantly networking and stuff.
So, you know, like, I might use it for that because HubSpot sucks for, like, the user experience.
But maybe you figure something else that we maybe missed, but I wasn't deeply involved in that decision, but I was kind of annoyed, but at the same time understood, you know.
Well, when I test it out, if I, I'm going to hit their API endpoints because I just will develop a basically UI on top of it or to integrate with our system.
And, like, I will, there's got to be timestamps in there.
Wow.
Please tell me if you find it.
me.
I will email you and like, timestamps in Adio.
And you'll be like, all right, cool.
Cool.
All right.
So I, Steph, I think it was Steph that introduced us.
You used to be CMO at DeepGram or everything.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
But, well, before I jump in and do intro and stuff, I'd love to kind of hear your journey too.
And you're a background and I'm happy to do this.
Same too, in it helps.

Yeah, yeah, let's, so my family has been in the call center industry for 49 years, so I got my start being a call center agent when I was 16 years old, putting gas into my Ford Explorer, and I then did work as a cybersecurity researcher, did, actually before then I did, sorry, my computer's kind of freaked out, oh, we're good.

The, I, did IT work managing call center systems, phone switches, you know, we were doing before UiPath was a thing, we were like taking in emails, parsing them, dispatching them, like page duty style stuff for cell phone towers and stuff, saying, oh, you're, you they would send out alerts saying, the temperature in the data center is, you know, 78 degrees, you know, would send
We, you know, send out and, you know, then we would dispatch out.
Anyway, so did some of that work, left the call center space for a while, joined a startup.
There was a cryptography company in Atlanta.
Fast forward, joined Datadog, did work there, launched their first cybersecurity product, left after we had four products because I was just dog tired.
Um, and, uh, so, uh, I recuperated for a month and then decided to start my own thing because I wasn't tired enough.
So, here I am.
I was doing, I started out as a lifestyle business, um, three years ago doing, uh, cybersecurity for call centers.
Um, and that was before OpenAI released, uh, 3.5, uh, and, and then everything changed.
Um, and last November, I was still.
They did cybersecurity stuff, and I became known as the technologist in the call center.
Specifically, do you know telefinancing services?
Are you familiar with them?
Mm-hmm.
A bit.
It's just a market sizing.
It's a $1.5 billion revenue industry.
It's smaller compared to larger BPOs, larger call centers.
But I know everyone in that industry, and everyone knows me and sees me as a technologist and, like, kind of a pusher and breaker of things in the industry.
So someone called me up back in November and be like, hey, can you build us an AI agent, voice agent, and everything we've tried on the market sucks?
Please build us one that works well.

I was like, okay, I got, what I decided to do is teach a course on how to build your own AI agents, because I was like, I will get one-on-one time with customers, call centers, and we'll figure out, we'll build it to their use cases and kind of see what happens.
First tried it on Twilio, I mean, in the worst possible way I can think possible, looking back at everything, but it was me being naive and seeing how to do it.
And we got some working, but there were a lot of problems with it.
Then we switched over to SignalWire because I had met those guys.
A lot of the call centers run free switches in their system, and SignalWire is the maintainer of that open source project.

And then at the end of the course, everyone had AI agents that were up and running, and they were running successfully, and they needed things like, hey, how do we integrate this with our customer systems?
How do we bill our customers for this?
Because answering services are all outsourced, so they have to turn that AI agent revenue or turn that AI agent work into revenue that they bill their customers for.
So how do they integrate with their billing platforms?
How do you do warm handoff into the call center platforms when you need a human agent?
All those things.
So I was like, all right, I'll build this.
Let's go.
Let's build it.
So we did.
We were here today.
We have integrations with the two incumbents in the space.
We can send data into their APIs.
We have other ways to extract data if they don't have API access because they, for whatever reason, don't have it.
We have, you know, the ability to just build some, you know, build voice agents.
It's very, at a technology level, it's very similar to a BAPI or a live kit or a, you know, out of the box editor where you can just create your agents.
But where we're going, what we're doing is, you know, building very like agentic loops and conversational stuff.
So you can be like, what do you want to do today?
I need to add a new customer that's a property management company.
Okay.
Okay.
What are their top, you know, what are their use cases?
Do you have any call?
Recordings.
Okay, we transcribed those.
Okay, we see these common paths.
Let us build these for you.
And we build them all out.
And we can do it, you know, with no solution engineer involved.
And it just builds it.
And lately, the work I've been doing is all around AI evaluations and saying, okay, we have the system prompt done.
I can actually demo to you.
I have it where I can show it to you in just a sec.
That's awesome, dude.
Well, yeah, I mean, I'd love to learn more because, you know, anyways, I'll tell you a little bit on my end too.
But that's definitely evals.
We definitely need to, there's a lot of stuff to figure out just for the belly.
Yeah.
So anyway, that's us.
We sell to outsourced call centers.
We'll sell to anyone that's in-house, but our go-to-market.
Like, you know, the tech underneath is going to be this similar for outsourced, but it's the go-to-market that I'm like, hey, this is our go-to-market.
I know this, I know this channel.
I know like people, my pipeline is
It's massive.
I just can't bring them on yet.
Cause we're not quite ready to just, you know, you know, put everyone on board.
Um, but like everyone, everyone in that space is like begging for access to this.
So that's, that's, So many questions to ask, but happy to do a quick background too, if you think would be helpful.
Yeah.
Just, I mean, just a, just a tiny bit, I guess.
Uh, yeah.
Yeah.
So, um, let's see, was just had a marketing growth of multiple companies.
Um, and, uh, early on career started building a lot of kind of like content programs to turn brands into more like publishers.
And so like, that was like kind of part of how it started.
And then was, uh, leading growth for HashiCorp went public service site and ended up going public, built a lot of the team there.
And then, uh, eventually it was that Skelly I was their first and last CMO for two years.
Um, I don't know if it was a good thing or a bad thing, but a crazy company.
Um, and, and then it was a deep ground CMO.

So for almost two years, and then part of, while I was there, I started building workflows to just, like, take a lot of my processes of how I would build content, and content strategy, and things like that, and start putting, like, humans in the loop.
We ended up 24 hours in your traffic, and growing like crazy, and so then I started teaching courses, workshops.
It was just five-hour Zoom, pretty much.
People paid $500, and I was just teaching them, and then after the first one, people were like, hey, can they just pay you to help me do strategy, workflow?
So adapting to these workflows, and then maybe some humans in the loop to help do the grunt work, and I was like, yeah, this one's good.
So I took on two, and then I did another workshop, that became six, did another workshop, that became 12, and I was like, oh, , we're almost a million and a half in revenue, I should quit my job.

And then I did, and partnered with my CTO and co-founder, who was, used to be, had a product in engineering at IFT, this, then that, IFT, back in the day, like, workflow automation, like, a precursor to Zapier, essentially.
And then...
And yeah, and then we decided to go all in on building a platform to power our services, which is mostly like content and marketing, you know.

And then what we built under the hood is mostly like a coding agent that takes a plan and creates workflows, whether it's deterministic or agentic with tool calling, knowledge base, you know, like retrieval, things like that.
And then a runtime layer and an execution layer.
And then almost kind of like an OS on top of that for our people to do the work for each client.
And kind of manage everything end to end.
And, and yeah, and then we just kept scaling.
And so we officially this week crossed a little over 10 million in revenue and we're like 70 employees and we were bootstrapped.
But in February, as we were growing, the numbers started getting kind of scary.
So I decided to raise a series A, you know, mostly like even in your profitable site, being bootstrapped is really risky as well.
You know, when you're growing fast.
so unless you start turning.
Down business like left and right, then it's kind of like, yeah, good luck.

And also it's really hard to attract like really, really, really, really good principal engineering talent from companies that are like more established because they're just like, okay, you're bootstrapped.
There's only one person that's going to benefit from, you know, this thing.
Well, it's you.
And so, yeah, we raised the Series A from Madrona and now we're, might raise the Series B next month or the following.
And and yeah, we're like 70 employees, which is insane.
I wish we were less, but we're lean and efficient, but it's kind of like, you know, it's always a nice balance.
But so it's been, it's been fun, crazy and hard and I have a two-year-old at home.
And so it's been, you know, two, two babies at the same time.
Nice.
It's fun to see rapid growth like that.
Like that's, that's, I that's always a fun thing.
I can't imagine being the CEO, but I was, I joined Datadog.
500 employees.
by the time I left, we had over 5,000.
It's like, it's really cool to like be a part of a, you just see like, like things catching fire and you're like, okay, like it's a good, it's a fun feeling, but I can't imagine.
Like I'm hoping I'm going to feel that very soon.
I feel it on the sense of like the demand.
I just haven't seen it in the forms of revenue yet.
So yeah, we're just early.
Cool.
I, I think you're probably onto something cause I, from being a deep gram, I started our startup program.

And so I definitely got to see a little over 300 different voice AI startups under the hood a little bit, you know, not fully, but you know, just enough to know, like the questions that they were asking, the things they were trying to figure out.
And we built a little Slack community between themselves.
And, and, and and then Vappy was a client.
We did some work from them this year.
We're not actively working with them right now.
I'll say, I'll
I got to see a little bit of that business and the things they're struggling with.
doing well, but also some of the things they're trying to figure out.
And so I think how you described that you went about it is exactly how I would have gone about it if I decided to do what you're doing.
So that's pretty cool, man.
Thanks, yeah.
I have a bunch of questions I could ask.
I guess, how can, let's figure out how we can, like, what's going be mutually beneficial for both of us?
Because there's probably things both ways that we can help.
I mean, we could also definitely chat just, you know, endless hours, I'm sure.
But I guess what's, initially, what's on your mind?
Is there things that I can help you with?
And then I can mention a couple of things on my side as well.
No, I think for me, like, honestly, Stephanie was just like, hey, you should talk.

Talked to Justin, and for me, I always believe in giving more than I take, and so anything that can be helpful, I think probably since you mentioned evals, it's probably anything that you're learning along the way there, and then maybe there's an opportunity to chat with my CTO as well, or one of our engineers or something, if there's things that you're like, whoa, I spent so much time on this, happy to share, and vice versa on our end as well.
We have, but other than that, just happy to connect and try to be of any value if I can.
Yeah, yeah.
I mean, for me right now, like, we're raising money, and some of the feedback we're getting, I'm trying to understand how much to weigh in, like, how much to, how much should I take?
So the target market we're selling into is answering services.
And that's, you know, if you look at it, it's a billion and a half revenue a year, and they charge on average like a buck and a half per minute.
So that gets you a billion minutes a year, right?
So if I say that today, 30% of those calls could be handled with AI, you know, you get to, okay, there's $300 million in revenue if you count the same price for the voice AI product.
But that's not really the case.
And then, but in two or three years, that number will go from 30% of serviceable to like 70%, right?
So like, I think we're going to see this grow in serviceable minutes.
But the VCs, you know, see this as a, one, some of them see it as a shrinking market, which...
I think it's the wrong way to think about it because they say things like, you know, you're selling to outsourced companies.
Why would you, like, why don't you go directly to their customers and sell to them, right?
Why don't you build a vertical SaaS and go sell that?
I'm like, well, I don't, so I get that part of it.
I mean, and that's pretty much it.
It's like, I'm like, also, this is just our start.
This is where we start.
And then we will, we will figure out where to go next.
Like, I have some inbound leads coming in that are, or in-house call centers.
So I guess my, my question for you, since you've kind of seen it at DeepGram and you've talked to these other startups, in, is, am I targeting like the right market?
Like, or like, is there, maybe how would you message it or maybe talk to VCs about that and like help them understand?
I mean, I guess first, tell me if I, tell me if I'm wrong.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I think I have a contrarian opinion, meaning it's not contrarian to you, contrarian to the VCs, it sounds like, that you're talking to, or agreeable to how I would approach it.
But the, what worked for us was, I'm solving my pain.
And, and, and so, whereas a lot of sets, a lot of tools, they go for the lowest common denominator and the least friction in the, in, you know, and that can work, sure.
But, but like, for me, I was like, give me the messy, give me the  that no one else wants to touch, you know, because it's hard.
And let me deliver as a service, and I'll  do it.
And, and then, but I also like charge as a service, too.
And because I'm taking on all the messy, I'll work backwards to what I need to build, and, and I'll, I'll get it done in any way possible.
And then over time, I'll figure out if there's a platform play and how the platform play should look like, and what are the components I can start to replace.

And now it might start with like honestly like coded, hard-coded like workflows with AI steps and then over time might become agentic loops and maybe I need to build some evals here or maybe I need to, you know, have a fallback system or maybe I need to have some like, you know, like figure out like maybe I use Vappy for this one little thing, you know, that might be okay for temporarily.
But then I figure out that, hey, Vappy didn't scale, like  this, like I'm going to do that or like, hey, how do I do retrieval?
Oh shoot, how do I do the moderation later?
How do I, right?

Like you're piecing all those things together versus if you start as like a Vappy, you're starting at such a like shallow level or if you're starting as a CSS or whatever that you're always going to be the like cool demo and that's what they're struggling.
Like they're trying to go enterprise, right?
Like so by you starting with the massiest one and then like the way I see it is like you're taking the Harvey AI approach.
don't know if you studied them or you should.
Go listen to every  interview from their CEO.

So, you know, and the way they approached was like, hey, I'm going go after the, like, the hardest  law firm out there that's going to give me the authority and the trust with everybody else in the ecosystem, and I'm going to actually co-sell with them.
So it's like, for you, you're going after a dying business that actually needs help to co-sell and be seen as an AI business.
So you can be their savior, but in some ways, and I think, right, like, I could be wrong here, you know more than me, but then that doesn't prevent you from going after any other opportunity.
That just goes after the hardest one to figure it out, but you're doing it with someone that still has money and is heavily invested in figuring out because it's existential, right?

And I think that's the struggle we saw with, like, the Five Nines of the world or even, like, Twilio or others that were customers of DeepGram, and even, like, and we had some that were, like, in-house contact center.
That were good customers, but man, like they had a massive engineering team, you know, so it's unrealistic to expect, I think, any in-house team to go figure this out and figure out what to replace.
And so they don't want it.
It's just like all the systems within contact centers, right?
Like they're so ingrained.
It's so hard to like pick apart different layers or, you know, so, and no one is just going to take this messy  thing and just one day just be like, like, that's not how it works, right?
Like, like, so they're chipping away at this lowest common denominator, trying to change their behavior.
And so, I don't know, like solve for revenue and sign up to do the messy work and then work backwards on what you need to build.
But then you're building after revenue.
So you don't have to worry about product market fit because people are paying you to figure it out.
And then you figure out what to build that will make you serving that services easier.
That's how I did it, right?
Like, so it sounds like a little bit familiar, similar pattern that, that.
It seems you started with Fathom.

Yeah, I think the one difference I've gone and I'm thinking that, you know, we might should change this and we started out doing those courses, we did that course, and then I switched to, okay, I know now basically what I need to build, I know I need this foundational thing that kind of looks like BAPI to give me the flexibility to do all this kind of stuff.
And then we're charging per minute for their calls on the usage, right?
Because that's how they think, because that's how they sell their product.
So we're selling it on the usage and we're not necessarily doing the professional services to get them there.
So for me, it's the thing that's forcing me to do is think about this as a, basically, how can I create like an agentic loop and just use AI to build this stuff more quickly?
So that I can do it as a very lean organization and we're not selling.

Professional services, but I think your point is, it's like, hey, go collect that cash up front, find the people who are willing to do that, and build that out for them, very, very bespoke for that one company, and then back away, and then look back and say, okay, this, we can turn it into a platform, this part is just going to have to remain custom for the foreseeable future or whatever.

Yeah, it's not that there's a right or wrong, it's just that if there's no network effects, and you need usage, need data, and you need, like, proof, and you need trust, and you need credibility, and you need, like, you know, money, you know, which, because then revenue means valuation, means, you know, capital, means leverage, whatever, I think there's a fine line of trying to be that one person, or super lean, or no services, and I don't know, like, I don't think services that ...
It's if you approach it from a good first principles, right?
Like, I don't know, there's things that I'm just like, I can just do a really good job hiring people and, you know, and I don't think it's that hard.
And if you say yes to one thing and you already have the agent, sure, it won't be full hands off, but it also won't be you hiring an army of expensive  engineers either, you know?
Like, right.
And so, so I don't think the alternative, if it means adding a zero threat, like the way I think about it is like, if you take what we do today, our average deal size is 200 grand a year.
And most of our deals close in like a week or two.
And, and we do like one or two 30 minute meeting.
Right.
And, and like our gross margin is like 70%.
So it's like, that's not a bad service to have, man.
If you're at 70%, you know, and your deal size is like that good, but it closes that fast because it's services.
It wouldn't close that fast if it was.
And if it was softer, I would be lucky if I was getting 200 to 2,000 a month instead, you know?
So it's like, I just removed two to three zeros from the , you know?
It's like, damn, that sucks.
Like, you know?
Yeah.
Yeah, the other part of the equation, and if we add zeros to this, it's kind of a forcing function to kind of solve this.

So maybe your answer's the same, but we have, the way we've, the way I thought about this at first, just to pick up just a little bit is, all right, we'll charge you per minute, and we'll charge you, we'll give you credits as every other AI company does, right?
But we're going to, in order for us to get cash flow, we say, okay, you can buy all your credits up front, and the more credits you buy, the cheaper discount you're going to get them.
So that gives us cash flow, right?
So we're like, hey, buy all your credits now, like, and like, you know, and we will start, we'll start implementing this with you, right?
So, but you may not, you may not.
Use those credits until three months, six months down the road.
The problem with that is that there's no urgency or incentive for the customer to deploy it faster and get it done.

So their urgency has to be that their customers are either leaving because they're going to a cheaper AI alternative or that they want to improve operational efficiency, which both are really good incentives to do it.
But we are seeing it's a little bit slower for them to go to market because they have to go out and, you know, have their – they're the outsource call center, right?
So they have to go in and, like, say, we're going to sell this new AI as a new line item instead of a human operator picking it up.
And they have to – are they going to charge the same price?
Or when call volume peaks, are they just going to use it then?
So they have to, like, kind of think through all these different scenarios.
So it's almost like I'm building this massive flywheel and it's just going to take really long.
Maybe not – maybe – but it'll take a longer.
For it to unload, rather than selling it directly to, you know, someone that is like an in-source call center, that they're replacing their humans with this.
I mean, if you have the relationships, right, like, do you have revenue now?
Tiny, but yes, tiny.
Is it tiny because you haven't gotten deployed?

So, we have every one of our initial customers deployed, their initial use cases, we want to test this internally, and we want to have our employees when they, because they're all, they have call centers, right?
So, when someone's calling out sick, they have people call out sick all the time.
So, we take that call for them, then we put it into their system, and then we notify their manager and let them know.
So, it's like just a very easy use case for them to test out on.
Now, we get those deployed, but that usage is really low.
So, then they have to take that and say, okay, now we're going to go and sell it into a property management company.
And it's a property management company.
It's going to, you know, we'll take these calls for them and front them with the AI.
then when the AI either can't handle it or, you know, they want to specifically send it to the human, say like a person stuck in the elevator or a person is locked out of their apartment.
They're like immediately go to a human and we just route them to a human.
If it's the maintenance request, you know, we take all that information on the AI.
So they are deploying it internally.
They have no problem with that.
But then it's like, how do they get the confidence to go in and deploy it and add the line items and all that stuff?
That's the issue.
Got it.
Can you compete with them?
Can you just like be the contact center?
And then you, you're a mix of humans in your own.
the, that's the, that's the question kind of like, like we could do, like, you could honestly, you could honestly do a PE rollup.
they're and they're not, and you're throwing them a lifeline and they're not  taking the lifeline fast enough.
They're like, hold on, let me go grab a cup of coffee first and I'll come grab the lifeline.
It's like.
Oh,  off then.
You know, it's like, honestly, that's where I'm at.
Like, then they deserve to die, right?
Like, unfortunately, but fortunately.
And so you're, I mean, if I was in your shoes, I don't have all the context I've got.
I have to shortcut a little bit, you know, so it could be wrong, is like, be aggressive and be like, look, here's the deal.
You're going to die.
Like, there is no scenario where you don't cease to exist.
So you have two choices.
You can be the first one in your entire industry to be 100% AI native and adopt and embrace that.
And we can co-sell and we can both be wildly  successful or, like, you can do nothing and someone else will.
So it's your choice.
But what you can do is be at 0.1% of your call volume with AI and be charged with the same thing.
What you can do is figure out as aggressively as possible how to eat your own business.
So you can be the first one to charge half of the cost to your customers.
cost cost cost of of you.
And then eat the whole industry.
And then you have an actual chance of being successful and still making some money for the next 10 years.
You know, like some version of that.

And then if like after the Hail Mary that doesn't work, then it's like I would just like if you have any capital, anything at all, it's just like start as a small niche, you know, like thing of just being like, you know what, I'm going to be a little contact center.
I'm going to go, you know, cold call to 10 property management companies and earn their business for contact center.
And, or just one that has a hundred locations and be like, we'll do it for one 10th of the cost.
And you eat up that cost until you figure out how to automate it.
And then you, you know, or you  say like, I'll do it almost for free.
Let me handle all your calls, you know, like, and then like you just build a business from there.

And then once you have one or two of those, then, then I think, but I just don't know like the, the details on like how desperately you need capital versus how much you can, you know, go, go figure that out.
Because.
It gives you a much better leverage on everything else, you know?
Yeah.
We're actually seeing General Catalyst did something like this in this market.
They went out and bought a call center.
It's called Crescendo.
And they're doing – we haven't seen them come up in any of our – our customers haven't mentioned losing any customers to Crescendo.
But it is actively being done by GC.
And, you know, who knows who's next?
So maybe buying a small one is better, you think?
Yeah, because you get – with that, at least, you get the – like, you get the existing customers, and you can just roll it out to them.
And, like, your margins – you're just going immediately get to improve your margins, you know, if done well, right?
mean, like – Do you have any contacts on any of these people that you'll be like – if you say, hey, give me 10% of your calls for a month, and let's measure based on this.
And if after this, like, doesn't work, you don't pay me anything.

## Meeting Purpose

[Explore potential collaboration and share insights on AI-powered contact center solutions and content generation.](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Justin's company, Relay Hawk, is developing AI agents for outsourced call centers, facing challenges in market adoption and scaling.](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Marcel's company, growthx.ai, has successfully scaled AI-powered content generation services with a focus on high-quality outputs and efficient human-AI collaboration.](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Both discussed the importance of solving for revenue, potentially pivoting business models, and the complexities of AI evaluation and fine-tuning.](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Marcel suggested more aggressive strategies for Justin to drive adoption and revenue, including potentially acquiring a small call center or buying out specific customer contracts.](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1730.0)

## Topics

### Relay Hawk's Business Model and Challenges

  - [Developing AI agents for outsourced call centers, particularly in the answering services market ($1.5B industry)](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Charging per minute for AI agent usage, with upfront credit purchases for cash flow](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1547.0)
  - [Facing slow adoption from existing call centers due to fear of revenue loss](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1227.0)
  - [Considering pivot to becoming a full-service AI-powered call center](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1723.0)

### growthx.ai's Success and Approach

  - [Scaled to $10M+ revenue, 70 employees, bootstrapped then raised Series A](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=695.0)
  - [Focus on solving complex, "messy" problems in content generation for high-value clients](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1504.0)
  - [Developed a platform with coding agents, workflow engines, and evaluation systems](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=668.0)
  - [70% gross margins on primarily service-based revenue model](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1487.0)

### AI Evaluation and Quality Assurance

  - [Relay Hawk: Post-conversation evals and synthetic data generation for testing](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=2365.0)
  - [growthx.ai: Multi-step evaluation process including fact-checking, quality assessment, and human editing evaluation](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=2496.0)
  - [Both exploring ways to make evaluation more objective and efficient](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=2496.0)

### Fine-tuning and Model Optimization

  - [Discussion on potential benefits of fine-tuning models to reduce context bloat and improve efficiency](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=2872.0)
  - [Importance of structuring data collection for potential future fine-tuning efforts](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=3031.0)

### Market Strategy and Revenue Generation

  - [Marcel advised Justin to be more aggressive in approach, potentially buying out specific customer contracts or acquiring a small call center](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1730.0)
  - [Emphasis on solving for revenue first, then optimizing technology and processes](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1297.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Justin to consider more aggressive strategies for driving adoption and revenue, including potential acquisitions or contract buyouts](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=2028.0)
  - [Explore fine-tuning possibilities for Relay Hawk's AI models to improve efficiency](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=2872.0)
  - [Justin to refine Relay Hawk's value proposition to emphasize improved customer experience, not just cost-cutting](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=1564.0)
  - [Potential follow-up on AI evaluation techniques, with Marcel offering to connect Justin with growthx.ai's CTO for further discussion](https://fathom.video/share/xPVw9otZcsvwYRzvKy11VUfzyJUD55Ku?tab=summary&timestamp=2330.0)
